Let's nail this down systematically. Starting with the foundation everything else depends on.

---

**Q1: What's your target platform — web only, or do you also want mobile (iOS/Android)?**

My recommendation: **Web only (responsive design).** A responsive web app covers mobile browsers without the overhead of native builds, app store submissions, or separate codebases. You can always wrap it in a PWA later if needed. Going cross-platform native from the start would triple the scope before you've even built a single game.

My recommendation: **Next.js (React) + Tailwind CSS for frontend, with Supabase as the backend (auth + database).** This gives you:
- React for component-based game UIs (each game is a self-contained component)
- Supabase handles login/signup out of the box + PostgreSQL for score storage + row-level security so users only see their own data
- Tailwind for fast, consistent styling without writing CSS from scratch
- All of this is free tier friendly for a personal/portfolio project

content.text:
**Q4: Your dashboard wants percentile ("where he stands") — but percentile requires comparing against a population. Whose scores form that population: only registered users, or do you also want to seed/include some baseline data?**

My recommendation: **Compute percentile against all registered users' scores only, and don't worry about looking sparse early on.** Here's the reasoning:
- Guests play locally and never hit your DB, so they can't be part of any global aggregate — only logged-in users contribute.
- Early on you'll have very few users, so percentiles will be statistically meaningless (being "90th percentile" out of 4 people is noise). That's fine for an MVP — it's honest, and it self-corrects as users grow.
- **Avoid seeding fake data.** It's tempting to inject synthetic scores to make percentiles look real, but it corrupts your actual statistics and you'll never cleanly remove it later. If you want a believable baseline, instead pull the *real* published distributions from humanbenchmark.com (they show histograms) and display those as a static "global average" reference line, clearly separate from your own computed percentile.

So: percentile = computed live from your registered users; optionally show a static reference distribution for context. Does that match what you pictured, or did you imagine guests' scores counting too?

content.text:
Got it — a bell curve with the user's score marked against the average is cleaner and more intuitive than a percentile number. That still needs aggregate stats, so let's pin down the inputs.

**Q5: On that bell curve, what single number represents the *user* — their best score for that game, their most recent score, or their average across all their attempts?**

My recommendation: **Their best score.** That's what Human Benchmark uses and it's what players emotionally care about ("my best reaction time is 215ms"). Average gets dragged down by fumbled attempts and feels punishing; latest is noisy. Best score is the natural "this is my standing" number, and it's a clean single point to drop onto the curve next to the population average.

One caveat to flag: "best" means opposite things per game — lowest ms for Reaction Time, highest score for Verbal Memory. We'll store a per-game flag for sort direction so the dashboard knows which way "better" is. Sound right?

content.text:
Let me reconcile this with the bell curve, because you've now introduced "average" as the headline number.

So the picture is:
- **End of a game round:** show the score the user just got (this round's result).
- **Dashboard, per game:** show the user's *average* score across their attempts.

**Q6: On the bell curve, which of the user's numbers gets marked — their average (matching the dashboard headline) or their best?**

My recommendation: **Mark the user's average, and make the curve's center the global average across all registered users.** Since your dashboard headline for each game is now the user's average, the bell curve should plot that same number so the two agree — showing "your average vs everyone's average" tells a consistent story. Mixing them (dashboard shows average, curve marks best) would confuse the user about which number is "theirs."

One consequence worth naming: average is only meaningful after several attempts. With 1–2 plays it'll swing wildly. That's acceptable for an MVP as long as we show the attempt count next to it (e.g., "Avg 248ms over 7 plays") so the number has context. Agree?

message.content: yes mark the average, global average as center

content.text:
On to data retention, since "last 10 records" was specified only for guests.

**Q7: For logged-in users, do we keep their full history of every play, or also cap it (like last 10 per game)? And the dashboard average — computed over all-time or a recent window?**

My recommendation: **Keep full history for logged-in users (no cap), but compute the dashboard average over their last 10–20 attempts, not all-time.** Reasoning:
- Storage is trivially cheap (a score row is tiny), and keeping full history lets you later add "progress over time" charts — a natural feature for a benchmark app. Capping logged-in users to 10 throws that away permanently.
- But the *average* should use a recent window so it reflects current ability, not attempts from a year ago when they were new. An all-time average makes improvement invisible (your great recent scores get diluted by old bad ones).
- This keeps guest and logged-in averaging consistent: both average over ~last 10, the only difference is logged-in users retain the older rows too.

So: store everything, average the recent window. Good?

message.content: yes store everything, average recent window

content.text:
Now the guest-to-account transition, which is a UX decision people often forget until it bites them.

**Q8: When a guest who has been playing (with local records) logs in or signs up, do we import their local records into their new account, or discard them and start fresh?**

My recommendation: **Offer to import on first login, don't do it silently.** When a guest with local records authenticates, detect the local data and show a one-time prompt: "We found scores from your guest session — add them to your account?" Reasoning:
- Silently importing risks polluting a returning user's account with scores from a shared/public computer that aren't theirs.
- Silently discarding frustrates someone who just played 10 great rounds as a guest and then signed up specifically to keep them.
- A prompt respects both cases. After import (or decline), clear the local store to avoid re-importing on the next login.

One edge to note: imported guest scores would count toward the global average too once they're in the DB. That's fine and correct — they're real plays. Agree with the prompt approach?

content.text:
Now an architecture decision that affects how the bell curve performs.

**Q9: How do we compute the global average (and the spread needed to draw the bell curve) — query and aggregate all scores live on each dashboard load, or maintain a precomputed running aggregate per game?**

My recommendation: **Maintain a precomputed aggregate table, one row per game, updated incrementally.** Store `count`, `sum`, and `sum_of_squares` per game. From those three numbers you can derive both the mean and the standard deviation (the two things a bell curve needs) in O(1), without ever scanning the scores table. Update them via a database trigger (or RPC) whenever a score is inserted. Reasoning:
- Aggregating the full scores table on every dashboard load gets slower as you grow and wastes compute on data that barely changes.
- A normal/bell curve is fully defined by mean + standard deviation — `sum` gives you the mean, `sum_of_squares` gives you the variance. Three counters, cheap to update, instant to read.
- It also makes the dashboard a single fast read instead of a heavy scan.

Caveat: a true score distribution often isn't a perfect bell — reaction times are right-skewed. For an MVP, approximating it as a normal curve from mean+stddev is perfectly fine and looks clean. If you later want the real shape, you'd store a histogram (score buckets) instead. Want to keep the simple mean+stddev approach for now?

message.content: yes, keep mean+stddev for now

content.text:
Let's lock down auth specifics.

**Q10: What login methods do you want — email/password, magic link (passwordless email), social OAuth (Google/GitHub), or some combination?**

My recommendation: **Email/password + Google OAuth, both via Supabase Auth.** Reasoning:
- Email/password is the universal baseline everyone understands, and Supabase gives it to you with email verification built in.
- Google OAuth removes friction for the majority who'd rather click once than invent a password — and it's the single highest-converting social login for a general-audience app.
- Skip magic links for now: they add an email-deliverability dependency and a clunkier flow (go check inbox, come back) that hurts the "I just want to save my score" moment.
- Skip GitHub unless your audience is developer-heavy — for a general benchmark app, Google covers far more people.

So: email/password with verification, plus Google. Agree, or do you want to keep it to just one method for the MVP?

message.content: only social O auth

content.text:
Social OAuth only — simpler, no password management, no email verification flow to build.

**Q11: Which social providers — just Google, or also GitHub/others?**

My recommendation: **Google only for the MVP.** It covers the widest general audience with a single integration, and every additional provider is another OAuth app to register, configure, and maintain. You can add GitHub later in minutes via Supabase if your audience skews technical. Starting with one keeps your login screen clean and your setup minimal.

One thing to flag now so it's not a surprise later: with OAuth-only, a guest who never logs in stays fully local (localStorage), and the *only* path to contributing to global averages is signing in with Google. That's consistent with everything we've decided. Good with Google-only?

message.content: only google

content.text:
Let's define what a single stored "record" actually is, since it shapes the schema and the averages.

**Q12: Is one stored record = one complete game session, where a session may internally consist of several trials? For example, Human Benchmark's reaction test makes you click 5 times and reports the average of those 5 as your result.**

My recommendation: **One stored record = one full session result, where the per-trial mechanics live inside the game and only the final session number is persisted.** Reasoning:
- The user-facing "score" is the session result (e.g., "your reaction time: 230ms" = average of 5 clicks). Storing individual trials would bloat the table and complicate every average and curve calculation downstream.
- It keeps the schema uniform across all 5 games: each game, however it works internally, boils down to one numeric result per session. Reaction averages 5 clicks; Verbal Memory counts words until you fail; Number Memory counts digits reached — all collapse to a single number.
- Your "last 10 records" and "recent-window average" then naturally mean last 10 *sessions*, which is the intuitive reading.

So the games table of records is just: which game, the final numeric score, when, and who. Per-trial logic stays in the browser and is never stored. Sound right?

message.content: yes, one record per session

content.text:
A quick but important one, since scores feed a shared global average.

**Q13: These games run entirely in the browser, so a technical user could forge a score before it's saved (e.g., POST a fake 50ms reaction time). How much do we invest in preventing that for the MVP?**

My recommendation: **Minimal effort for the MVP — basic sanity bounds only, no real anti-cheat.** Concretely:
- Reject physiologically impossible values server-side (e.g., reaction time < 100ms, or a digit span of 50) via a simple range check in an RPC or a check constraint. This catches lazy fakes and accidental garbage.
- Don't build trial-replay verification, server-authoritative timing, or signed payloads — that's enormous effort for a hobby/portfolio benchmark app where there's no prize to cheat for.
- Accept that a determined user can still nudge their own numbers. Since the bell curve uses mean+stddev over many users, a handful of fakes barely move it, and individual cheating mostly just fools themselves.

So: range-check guardrails, nothing more. Agree, or do you care more about score integrity than a typical MVP would?

message.content: keep it minimal

content.text:
Here's an unresolved branch about who sees what.

**Q14: Do guests get the dashboard too — their local average plotted on the bell curve — or is the dashboard a logged-in-only feature?**

My recommendation: **Give guests a dashboard too, powered by their localStorage records.** Reasoning:
- The global average and stddev come from your precomputed aggregate table, which can be read publicly (no auth needed — it's anonymous aggregate stats). So you have everything needed to draw the curve for a guest: their local average as the marker, the public aggregate as the curve.
- It's a powerful conversion hook — a guest sees "your average vs the world" immediately, which is exactly the dopamine that makes them want to sign in to save it permanently.
- The only thing guests *can't* see is cross-device history or long-term progress charts — which becomes a natural "sign in with Google to keep these forever" upsell.

So: same dashboard UI for both; data source differs (localStorage for guests, DB for logged-in). Agree?

Let me pin the scope line between the guest dashboard and the full logged-in one.

**Q15: For the logged-in dashboard, is the MVP just "same bell curve + average, but persisted across devices," with progress-over-time charts deferred to later? Or do you want the historical progress chart in the MVP too?**

My recommendation: **Defer progress-over-time charts; MVP logged-in dashboard = the same bell curve + recent-window average as guests, just backed by their account.** Reasoning:
- We're already storing full history, so the data will be there when you want charts — nothing is lost by deferring.

So MVP logged-in = guest dashboard + cross-device persistence + contributes to global stats. Progress charts later. Good?

content.text:

**Q16: Visual direction — do you want to closely mirror Human Benchmark's look (minimalist, dark blue/teal, full-bleed colored game panels), or your own distinct theme?**

My recommendation: **Mirror the Human Benchmark aesthetic closely for the MVP.** Reasoning:
- That design is genuinely good for this use case: each game is a full-screen solid-color panel with one big instruction, zero distractions — which is exactly what timed reaction/memory tests need (no clutter to slow perception).
- It's a known-good target, so you spend zero time agonizing over design decisions and can focus on game logic and the data layer.
- "Distinct theme" is a real time sink with diminishing returns for a portfolio clone. You can re-skin later via Tailwind tokens once the app works.

One practical note: use Tailwind theme tokens (a small palette of per-game panel colors + neutral dark background) from the start, so a future re-skin is a token change, not a rewrite. Good with mirroring it?

content.text:
shadcn/ui + minimal monochrome — clean and modern. That creates one design tension worth resolving now.

**Q17: With a minimal/monochrome theme, how do the individual games get visual identity? Human Benchmark gives each game a distinct full-screen color (which also signals game states like "wait… now click!"). In a minimal palette, what carries that?**

My recommendation: **Keep the app chrome monochrome (shadcn neutrals), but allow a single accent color that changes per game *state*, not per game.** Reasoning:
- Reaction Time genuinely *needs* color-as-signal: red "wait" → green "go" is the clearest possible cue, and going monochrome there would hurt usability. So color earns its place when it conveys state.
- For game *identity*, lean on an icon + label per game card rather than a color, keeping the grid clean and on-theme.
- Define one accent token plus a small set of semantic state colors (wait/go/success/fail) in your Tailwind config. Everything else stays neutral. This honors "minimal" while not crippling the games that rely on color feedback.

So: monochrome chrome, icons for identity, semantic state colors where a game needs them. Support light + dark mode via shadcn? — and is that state-color exception acceptable to you?

content.text:
Let's handle the cold-start reality, since early on you'll have almost no global data.

**Q18: Before there's enough data to draw a meaningful bell curve (say, the first handful of users), what does the dashboard show for that game — the curve anyway, just the user's number, or a "not enough data yet" state?**

My recommendation: **Show the user's score plainly, and gate the curve behind a minimum sample threshold (e.g., ≥30 sessions for that game globally).** Below the threshold, replace the curve with a simple "Global comparison unlocks once more people have played" message; still show the user their own average and last-10. Reasoning:
- A bell curve drawn from 3 data points is statistically meaningless and actively misleading (a stddev from n=3 is noise) — showing it would undermine trust in the whole dashboard.
- The user's own average + recent history is valuable on day one regardless of global data, so the dashboard is never empty.
- A threshold gate makes the curve *earn* its appearance, and the "unlocks soon" framing is honest and even creates anticipation.

So: always show personal numbers; show the curve only past a sample-size threshold. Reasonable threshold to start, say 30? Or would you rather always attempt the curve?

- Vercel is the zero-config home for Next.js — push to git, it deploys, with previews per branch. No infra to manage.

aiTitle: Build human benchmark testing app with games and dashboard

content.text:
We've walked the whole tree. Here's the consolidated design we converged on.

## Human Benchmark Clone — Design Spec

### Stack & Infra
- **Frontend:** Next.js + TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui components
- **Backend:** Supabase (Postgres + Auth)
- **Hosting:** Vercel (app) + Supabase cloud, both free tiers
- **Theme:** Minimal monochrome, **dark mode default**; app chrome neutral; games identified by icon + label; semantic **state colors** (wait/go/success/fail) allowed where a game needs them (e.g., Reaction Time red→green)

### Games (all 5, built one at a time)
Reaction Time, Number Memory, Verbal Memory, Pattern Recognition, Reasoning.
- **One stored record = one full session result** (per-trial logic stays in the browser; only the final number persists). E.g., Reaction Time = average of 5 clicks → one record.
- Each game has config in code: `lower_is_better` flag, unit, and sane min/max **bounds** for server-side sanity checks.

### Users & Auth
- **Guest:** plays immediately, records stored in **localStorage, last 10 per game**
- **Auth:** **Google OAuth only** (Supabase Auth)
- **Logged-in:** **full history stored** (no cap), contributes to global stats
- **Guest → login:** on first sign-in with local data present, **prompt to import**; after import/decline, clear localStorage

### Dashboard
- **Per-game headline = user's average over a recent window (~last 10 sessions)**, shown with attempt count for context
- **Bell curve:** centered on **global average**; user's **average** marked on it
- Curve drawn from a **precomputed aggregate table** per game storing `count`, `sum`, `sum_of_squares` → mean + stddev in O(1), updated incrementally on insert (DB trigger/RPC)
- **Cold-start gate:** show user's score plainly always; **show the curve only once that game has ≥ ~30 global sessions**, otherwise a "comparison unlocks soon" message
- **Guests get a minimal dashboard too** (local average marked against the public global curve); aggregate table is publicly readable

# PRD: Human Benchmark Clone

**Status:** Ready for build
**Date:** 2026-06-23
**Owner:** vashutosh625@gmail.com

---

## Problem Statement

People want a quick, low-friction way to measure and track their cognitive abilities — reaction speed, memory, mental math, pattern recognition, and reasoning — and to understand how their performance compares to others. Existing options either require an account before you can play, don't persist your results, or show you a raw number with no context for whether it's good or bad. A user wants to (a) jump straight into a test without signing up, (b) keep a short history of how they're doing, (c) optionally sign in to keep results permanently across devices, and (d) see at a glance how their score stacks up against everyone else's.

## Solution

A responsive web app where any visitor can immediately play any of five cognitive tests as a guest, with their recent results saved locally. A single dashboard shows, per game, the user's average score and a bell curve positioning that average against the global average of all registered players. Users can sign in with Google to persist their full history across devices and contribute their scores to the global statistics. Guests who have been playing are prompted to import their local results when they first sign in.

The five tests: **Reaction Time, Number Memory, Verbal Memory, Pattern Recognition, Reasoning** — built and released one at a time, sharing a common scoring, storage, and dashboard pattern.

## User Stories

1. As a visitor, I want to start playing any game without creating an account, so that I can try the app with zero friction.
2. As a guest, I want my last 10 results per game saved on my device, so that I can see my recent performance without signing up.
3. As a guest, I want to see the score I just achieved immediately when a round ends, so that I get instant feedback.
4. As a guest, I want a minimal dashboard showing my average per game, so that I understand my typical performance.
5. As a guest, I want my average plotted on a bell curve against the global average, so that I can see how I compare to everyone else even before signing in.
6. As a guest who has played several rounds, I want to be prompted to import my local results when I first sign in, so that I don't lose the scores that motivated me to register.
7. As a guest, I want to decline importing my local results, so that scores from a shared/public computer don't pollute my account.
8. As a user, I want to sign in with my Google account, so that I can save my results without managing a password.
9. As a logged-in user, I want my complete play history stored, so that my results persist across devices and over time.
10. As a logged-in user, I want my dashboard average computed over my recent attempts, so that it reflects my current ability rather than old scores.
11. As a logged-in user, I want my scores to contribute to the global average, so that the comparison everyone sees is based on real players.
12. As a user, I want each game to show the attempt count alongside my average, so that I know how much data the average is based on.
13. As a user, I want the dashboard to always show my personal score even when there isn't enough global data, so that the dashboard is never empty.
14. As a user, I want the bell curve to appear only once a game has enough global plays, so that I'm not shown a statistically meaningless comparison.
15. As a user, I want a "comparison unlocks soon" message before the curve is available, so that I understand why it's not shown yet.
16. As a user playing Reaction Time, I want clear color cues (wait vs go), so that I can react correctly.
17. As a user, I want the app in dark mode by default with a clean minimal look, so that the experience is distraction-free.
18. As a user, I want each game identified by an icon and label, so that I can navigate the game grid easily.
19. As a user, I want the app to work on my phone's browser, so that I can play anywhere.
20. As a returning logged-in user, I want to see my up-to-date average and curve when I open the dashboard, so that my standing reflects my latest plays.
21. As a user, I want impossible scores rejected, so that the global statistics stay trustworthy.
22. As the product owner, I want games to share a common scoring and storage pattern, so that adding the remaining games is incremental and low-risk.

## Implementation Decisions

### Stack
- **Frontend:** Next.js + TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui component library.
- **Backend:** Supabase (Postgres + Auth).
- **Hosting:** Vercel (app) + Supabase cloud, both on free tiers.
- **Theme:** Minimal monochrome; dark mode default. App chrome stays neutral. Games are identified by icon + label, not per-game colors. Semantic **state colors** (wait / go / success / fail) are permitted where a game's usability depends on them (notably Reaction Time's red→green cue). State colors and the single accent are defined as theme tokens so a re-skin is a token change.

### Games & Scoring
- Five games, released one at a time: Reaction Time, Number Memory, Verbal Memory, Pattern Recognition, Reasoning.
- **One stored record = one full game session result.** Per-trial mechanics (e.g., Reaction Time averaging 5 clicks) live entirely in the browser; only the final session number is persisted.
- Each game declares config in code: a `lower_is_better` flag (true for Reaction Time, false for memory/reasoning games), a display unit, and min/max sanity bounds.
- Exact per-game trial counts and precise scoring formulas are deliberately decided at build time for each game (the cross-game *pattern* is fixed; the specifics are not).

### Users, Auth & Data
- **Guest play:** results stored in `localStorage`, capped at the **last 10 records per game**.
- **Auth:** **Google OAuth only**, via Supabase Auth. No email/password, no magic links, no other providers in MVP.
- **Logged-in storage:** **full history retained** (no cap), one row per session: which game, the numeric score, timestamp, user.
- **Dashboard average:** computed over a **recent window (~last 10 sessions)** for both guests and logged-in users; logged-in users simply retain older rows too. Average is shown with its attempt count.
- **Guest → account migration:** on first sign-in, if local records exist, **prompt the user to import** them. After import or decline, clear `localStorage` to prevent re-import. Imported scores count toward global stats (they are real plays).

### Global Statistics & Bell Curve
- A **precomputed aggregate table, one row per game**, stores `count`, `sum`, and `sum_of_squares`. Mean and standard deviation are derived in O(1); the scores table is never scanned for the dashboard.
- The aggregate row is updated **incrementally on score insert** (DB trigger or RPC).
- The aggregate table is **publicly readable** (anonymous aggregate stats), so guests can render the bell curve from their local average against the global mean/stddev.
- The bell curve is approximated as a **normal distribution from mean + stddev** for the MVP (acknowledged that real distributions, e.g. reaction times, are right-skewed; histogram-shaped curves are a later option).
- The user's marker on the curve is their **recent-window average** (matching the dashboard headline), and the curve is centered on the **global average**.

### Cold-Start Gating
- The dashboard **always shows the user's own score/average** regardless of global data volume.
- The **bell curve is gated behind a minimum global sample size** (start at **≥ 30 sessions** for that game). Below the threshold, show a "Global comparison unlocks once more people have played" message instead of the curve.

### Integrity
- **Minimal anti-cheat:** server-side range/sanity checks reject physiologically impossible values (e.g., reaction time below a floor, absurd digit spans) via RPC validation or check constraints. No replay verification, server-authoritative timing, or signed payloads.

## Testing Decisions

A good test here asserts **external behavior, not implementation details** — given inputs, the observable result — so internals can be refactored freely. Since this is a greenfield repo with no prior art, the following seams are proposed, favoring the fewest, highest seams:

1. **Scoring/statistics seam (preferred, highest-value, pure functions):** The score-to-record transformation and the aggregate math (deriving mean and standard deviation from `count`/`sum`/`sum_of_squares`, and computing a normal-curve position for a given average) are pure functions with no I/O. Test them directly with table-driven cases: known inputs → known mean/stddev/curve position, including edge cases (n=0, n=1, all-equal scores, `lower_is_better` direction). This is the densest seam and should carry most coverage.
2. **Sanity-bounds validation seam:** The server-side validation that accepts/rejects a submitted score for a given game's bounds is a single decision function. Test boundary values (just inside/outside min and max) and impossible inputs.
3. **Guest storage seam:** The localStorage record store (append a result, enforce last-10 cap per game, read back for the dashboard, clear on import). Test the cap behavior and per-game isolation against a mocked storage interface.
4. **Migration seam:** The guest-import decision flow (detect local data present → import vs decline → clear local store). Test that decline preserves account state and clears local data, and import merges then clears.

Integration with Supabase Auth/DB (Google OAuth round-trip, trigger-based aggregate updates) is verified at a thin boundary; prefer keeping business logic out of the DB layer so the pure seams above carry the load.

## Out of Scope

- Native mobile apps (web/responsive only).
- Email/password auth, magic links, and any OAuth provider other than Google.
- Percentile/ranking numbers (replaced by the bell-curve comparison).
- Progress-over-time / historical charts (v2 — full history is stored so the data will be ready).
- Real histogram-shaped global distributions (MVP uses normal-curve approximation).
- Server-authoritative timing, replay verification, signed score payloads, or any non-minimal anti-cheat.
- Seeding synthetic/fake scores to make percentiles or curves look populated.
- Cross-device history for guests.

## Further Notes

- Games ship one at a time; the shared session-record + dashboard + aggregate pattern is built once and reused, so each new game is an incremental addition.
- Early on, global comparisons will be sparse by design — the cold-start gate keeps the app honest rather than showing meaningless curves.
- The ~30-session curve threshold and the ~10-session averaging window are starting values and can be tuned after observing real usage.
- Storing full history (despite averaging only a recent window) is a deliberate investment so v2 progress charts require no schema change.

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project status

This repo is **pre-scaffold**. The only committed files are `PRD.md` (the full product spec) and `.gitignore`. There is no `package.json`, no source tree, and therefore **no build/lint/test commands yet** — do not invent them. When scaffolding, the agreed stack is Next.js + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui (frontend) and Supabase (Postgres + Auth). Read `PRD.md` before making architectural decisions; it is the source of truth for scope and the deferred-vs-MVP line.

Once the project is scaffolded, update this file with the real `dev` / `build` / `lint` / `test` commands and how to run a single test.

## What this app is

FrontalLobe is a Human Benchmark clone: five cognitive tests (Reaction Time, Number Memory, Verbal Memory, Pattern Recognition, Reasoning) that anyone can play as a guest, with an optional Google sign-in to persist results. A single dashboard shows, per game, the user's recent-window average plotted on a bell curve against the global average of all registered players.

## Architecture (the load-bearing decisions)

These are the cross-cutting invariants that span multiple files — get them right and the rest follows from the PRD.

- **One stored record = one full game session result.** Per-trial mechanics (e.g. Reaction Time averaging 5 clicks) live entirely in the browser; only the final numeric result per session is persisted. Every game collapses to a single number, which keeps storage, averaging, and the curve uniform across all five games.

- **Per-game config lives in code**, not the DB: a `lower_is_better` flag (true only for Reaction Time), a display unit, and min/max sanity bounds. "Better" means opposite directions per game, so anything ranking or positioning a score must consult this flag.

- **Two storage backends, one dashboard.** Guests store their **last 10 records per game in `localStorage`** (hard cap). Logged-in users store **full history** in Supabase (no cap). The dashboard UI is the same for both; only the data source differs. Averages for both are computed over a **recent window (~last 10 sessions)** — full history is retained for logged-in users purely to enable v2 progress charts without a schema change.

- **Global stats are a precomputed aggregate, never a table scan.** A single row per game holds `count`, `sum`, and `sum_of_squares`, updated incrementally on insert (DB trigger/RPC). Mean and standard deviation derive from those three counters in O(1). The bell curve is a **normal-distribution approximation from mean + stddev** — do not scan the scores table to draw it. This aggregate row is **publicly readable** so guests can render the curve from their local average.

- **The user's marker on the curve is their recent-window average** (matching the dashboard headline number), and the curve is centered on the global average. Keep these two numbers the same metric — never plot "best" while the headline shows "average".

- **Cold-start gate:** always show the user's own score/average; show the bell curve only once that game has **≥ ~30 global sessions**, otherwise a "comparison unlocks soon" message. The 30-session threshold and the 10-session averaging window are tunable starting values.

- **Guest → account migration:** on first Google sign-in, if local records exist, **prompt to import** (never silent). After import or decline, clear `localStorage` so it can't re-import. Imported scores count toward global stats.

- **Integrity is minimal by design:** server-side range/sanity checks reject physiologically impossible values (via RPC validation or check constraints). No replay verification, server-authoritative timing, or signed payloads — do not add these without revisiting the PRD.

## Auth

Google OAuth **only**, via Supabase Auth. No email/password, no magic links, no other providers in MVP. Guests who never sign in stay fully local and never contribute to global stats.

## Testing approach (per PRD)

Test **external behavior, not implementation details.** The highest-value seam is the **pure scoring/statistics functions** (record transformation; deriving mean/stddev from the three counters; computing curve position respecting `lower_is_better`) — carry most coverage there with table-driven cases including edge cases (n=0, n=1, all-equal scores). Other seams: sanity-bounds validation, the guest localStorage store (cap + per-game isolation), and the migration decision flow. Keep business logic out of the Supabase layer so these pure seams stay testable.

## Conventions

- **Theme:** minimal monochrome, **dark mode default**. App chrome is neutral; games are identified by icon + label, not per-game colors. **State colors** (wait/go/success/fail) are permitted only where a game's usability needs them (notably Reaction Time's red→go cue). Define accent and state colors as theme tokens so a re-skin is a token change.
- **Games ship one at a time.** Build the shared session-record + dashboard + aggregate pattern once; each new game is an incremental addition, not a new subsystem.

## Out of scope (don't build without revisiting the PRD)

Native mobile apps; non-Google auth; percentile/ranking numbers; progress-over-time charts (v2); histogram-shaped distributions; non-minimal anti-cheat; seeding fake scores; cross-device history for guests.

file.content:
# PRD: Human Benchmark Clone

**Status:** Ready for build
**Date:** 2026-06-23
**Owner:** vashutosh625@gmail.com

---

## Problem Statement

People want a quick, low-friction way to measure and track their cognitive abilities — reaction speed, memory, mental math, pattern recognition, and reasoning — and to understand how their performance compares to others. Existing options either require an account before you can play, don't persist your results, or show you a raw number with no context for whether it's good or bad. A user wants to (a) jump straight into a test without signing up, (b) keep a short history of how they're doing, (c) optionally sign in to keep results permanently across devices, and (d) see at a glance how their score stacks up against everyone else's.

## Solution

A responsive web app where any visitor can immediately play any of five cognitive tests as a guest, with their recent results saved locally. A single dashboard shows, per game, the user's average score and a bell curve positioning that average against the global average of all registered players. Users can sign in with Google to persist their full history across devices and contribute their scores to the global statistics. Guests who have been playing are prompted to import their local results when they first sign in.

The five tests: **Reaction Time, Number Memory, Verbal Memory, Pattern Recognition, Reasoning** — built and released one at a time, sharing a common scoring, storage, and dashboard pattern.

## User Stories

1. As a visitor, I want to start playing any game without creating an account, so that I can try the app with zero friction.
2. As a guest, I want my last 10 results per game saved on my device, so that I can see my recent performance without signing up.
3. As a guest, I want to see the score I just achieved immediately when a round ends, so that I get instant feedback.
4. As a guest, I want a minimal dashboard showing my average per game, so that I understand my typical performance.
5. As a guest, I want my average plotted on a bell curve against the global average, so that I can see how I compare to everyone else even before signing in.
6. As a guest who has played several rounds, I want to be prompted to import my local results when I first sign in, so that I don't lose the scores that motivated me to register.
7. As a guest, I want to decline importing my local results, so that scores from a shared/public computer don't pollute my account.
8. As a user, I want to sign in with my Google account, so that I can save my results without managing a password.
9. As a logged-in user, I want my complete play history stored, so that my results persist across devices and over time.
10. As a logged-in user, I want my dashboard average computed over my recent attempts, so that it reflects my current ability rather than old scores.
11. As a logged-in user, I want my scores to contribute to the global average, so that the comparison everyone sees is based on real players.
12. As a user, I want each game to show the attempt count alongside my average, so that I know how much data the average is based on.
13. As a user, I want the dashboard to always show my personal score even when there isn't enough global data, so that the dashboard is never empty.
14. As a user, I want the bell curve to appear only once a game has enough global plays, so that I'm not shown a statistically meaningless comparison.
15. As a user, I want a "comparison unlocks soon" message before the curve is available, so that I understand why it's not shown yet.
16. As a user playing Reaction Time, I want clear color cues (wait vs go), so that I can react correctly.
17. As a user, I want the app in dark mode by default with a clean minimal look, so that the experience is distraction-free.
18. As a user, I want each game identified by an icon and label, so that I can navigate the game grid easily.
19. As a user, I want the app to work on my phone's browser, so that I can play anywhere.
20. As a returning logged-in user, I want to see my up-to-date average and curve when I open the dashboard, so that my standing reflects my latest plays.
21. As a user, I want impossible scores rejected, so that the global statistics stay trustworthy.
22. As the product owner, I want games to share a common scoring and storage pattern, so that adding the remaining games is incremental and low-risk.

## Implementation Decisions

### Stack
- **Frontend:** Next.js + TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui component library.
- **Backend:** Supabase (Postgres + Auth).
- **Hosting:** Vercel (app) + Supabase cloud, both on free tiers.
- **Theme:** Minimal monochrome; dark mode default. App chrome stays neutral. Games are identified by icon + label, not per-game colors. Semantic **state colors** (wait / go / success / fail) are permitted where a game's usability depends on them (notably Reaction Time's red→green cue). State colors and the single accent are defined as theme tokens so a re-skin is a token change.

### Games & Scoring
- Five games, released one at a time: Reaction Time, Number Memory, Verbal Memory, Pattern Recognition, Reasoning.
- **One stored record = one full game session result.** Per-trial mechanics (e.g., Reaction Time averaging 5 clicks) live entirely in the browser; only the final session number is persisted.
- Each game declares config in code: a `lower_is_better` flag (true for Reaction Time, false for memory/reasoning games), a display unit, and min/max sanity bounds.
- Exact per-game trial counts and precise scoring formulas are deliberately decided at build time for each game (the cross-game *pattern* is fixed; the specifics are not).

### Users, Auth & Data
- **Guest play:** results stored in `localStorage`, capped at the **last 10 records per game**.
- **Auth:** **Google OAuth only**, via Supabase Auth. No email/password, no magic links, no other providers in MVP.
- **Logged-in storage:** **full history retained** (no cap), one row per session: which game, the numeric score, timestamp, user.
- **Dashboard average:** computed over a **recent window (~last 10 sessions)** for both guests and logged-in users; logged-in users simply retain older rows too. Average is shown with its attempt count.
- **Guest → account migration:** on first sign-in, if local records exist, **prompt the user to import** them. After import or decline, clear `localStorage` to prevent re-import. Imported scores count toward global stats (they are real plays).

### Global Statistics & Bell Curve
- A **precomputed aggregate table, one row per game**, stores `count`, `sum`, and `sum_of_squares`. Mean and standard deviation are derived in O(1); the scores table is never scanned for the dashboard.
- The aggregate row is updated **incrementally on score insert** (DB trigger or RPC).
- The aggregate table is **publicly readable** (anonymous aggregate stats), so guests can render the bell curve from their local average against the global mean/stddev.
- The bell curve is approximated as a **normal distribution from mean + stddev** for the MVP (acknowledged that real distributions, e.g. reaction times, are right-skewed; histogram-shaped curves are a later option).
- The user's marker on the curve is their **recent-window average** (matching the dashboard headline), and the curve is centered on the **global average**.

### Cold-Start Gating
- The dashboard **always shows the user's own score/average** regardless of global data volume.
- The **bell curve is gated behind a minimum global sample size** (start at **≥ 30 sessions** for that game). Below the threshold, show a "Global comparison unlocks once more people have played" message instead of the curve.

### Integrity
- **Minimal anti-cheat:** server-side range/sanity checks reject physiologically impossible values (e.g., reaction time below a floor, absurd digit spans) via RPC validation or check constraints. No replay verification, server-authoritative timing, or signed payloads.

## Testing Decisions

A good test here asserts **external behavior, not implementation details** — given inputs, the observable result — so internals can be refactored freely. Since this is a greenfield repo with no prior art, the following seams are proposed, favoring the fewest, highest seams:

1. **Scoring/statistics seam (preferred, highest-value, pure functions):** The score-to-record transformation and the aggregate math (deriving mean and standard deviation from `count`/`sum`/`sum_of_squares`, and computing a normal-curve position for a given average) are pure functions with no I/O. Test them directly with table-driven cases: known inputs → known mean/stddev/curve position, including edge cases (n=0, n=1, all-equal scores, `lower_is_better` direction). This is the densest seam and should carry most coverage.
2. **Sanity-bounds validation seam:** The server-side validation that accepts/rejects a submitted score for a given game's bounds is a single decision function. Test boundary values (just inside/outside min and max) and impossible inputs.
3. **Guest storage seam:** The localStorage record store (append a result, enforce last-10 cap per game, read back for the dashboard, clear on import). Test the cap behavior and per-game isolation against a mocked storage interface.
4. **Migration seam:** The guest-import decision flow (detect local data present → import vs decline → clear local store). Test that decline preserves account state and clears local data, and import merges then clears.

Integration with Supabase Auth/DB (Google OAuth round-trip, trigger-based aggregate updates) is verified at a thin boundary; prefer keeping business logic out of the DB layer so the pure seams above carry the load.

## Out of Scope

- Native mobile apps (web/responsive only).
- Email/password auth, magic links, and any OAuth provider other than Google.
- Percentile/ranking numbers (replaced by the bell-curve comparison).
- Progress-over-time / historical charts (v2 — full history is stored so the data will be ready).
- Real histogram-shaped global distributions (MVP uses normal-curve approximation).
- Server-authoritative timing, replay verification, signed score payloads, or any non-minimal anti-cheat.
- Seeding synthetic/fake scores to make percentiles or curves look populated.
- Cross-device history for guests.

## Further Notes

- Games ship one at a time; the shared session-record + dashboard + aggregate pattern is built once and reused, so each new game is an incremental addition.
- Early on, global comparisons will be sparse by design — the cold-start gate keeps the app honest rather than showing meaningless curves.
- The ~30-session curve threshold and the ~10-session averaging window are starting values and can be tuned after observing real usage.
- Storing full history (despite averaging only a recent window) is a deliberate investment so v2 progress charts require no schema change.

## What to build
